perturbation.original_text: Diplomat agrees that it will obtain, at its own expense, product liability insurance from a recognized insurance company which is qualified to do business in the State of California providing adequate protection (at least in the amount of $ ) for KI, Inc., KI and Diplomat against any claims, suits, loss or damage arising out of any alleged defects in the products. As proof of such insurance, a fully paid certificate of insurance naming KI, Inc. and KI as an insured party will be submitted to KI, Inc. by Diplomat for KI, Inc.'s prior approval before any product is distributed or sold, and at the latest within thirty (30) days after the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: Diplomat agrees that it will obtain, at its own expense, product liability insurance from a recognized insurance company which is qualified to do business in the State of California providing adequate protection (at least in the amount of $ ) for KI, Inc., KI and Diplomat against any claims, suits, loss or damage arising out of any alleged defects in the products. As proof of such insurance, a fully paid certificate of insurance naming KI, Inc. and KI as an insured party will be submitted to KI, Inc. by Diplomat only upon request from KI Inc., if KI, Inc. deems it necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring submission of the insurance certificate only upon request from KI, Inc., rather than mandating it within a specified timeframe (30 days), creates non-compliance because California law requires businesses to maintain proof of insurance and provide it upon request, making the original 'within 30 days' a more specific and enforceable timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Regulations, Title 16, Division 8, Article 7, § 1761
perturbation.law_explanation: 16 CCR § 1761 requires businesses to maintain and provide proof of insurance. Removing the 30-day deadline in the contract makes it ambiguous when proof must be provided, potentially allowing Diplomat to delay or avoid providing proof, undermining the purpose of the regulation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the original contract by making the submission of the insurance certificate conditional upon KI Inc.'s request, removing the mandatory 30-day timeframe. This creates an inconsistency, weakening the assurance that proof of insurance will be promptly available. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change creates non-compliance because California law requires businesses to maintain proof of insurance and provide it upon request. The original 30-day requirement provides a more specific and enforceable timeframe. The Law Explanation reinforces this, stating that removing the deadline makes it ambiguous when proof must be provided, potentially undermining the regulation. The absence of valid scraped snippets does not negate the contradiction, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations adequately highlight the conflict with general principles of maintaining and providing proof of insurance.

perturbation.original_text: Failure to reject any product, carton, container, or packing or wrapping within 15 days of receipt of such item or items by KI, Inc. shall be deemed an acceptance of the quality and style of such item or items.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure to reject any product, carton, container, or packing or wrapping within 3 days of receipt of such item or items by KI, Inc. shall be deemed an acceptance of the quality and style of such item or items.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe to 3 days from 15 days for product samples may be considered unreasonably short. California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act reasonably in exercising their contractual rights. A 3-day review period might not allow sufficient time for proper evaluation, potentially harming KI, Inc.'s ability to protect its brand and could be deemed a breach of this covenant.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201 defines 'agreement' and how it is interpreted. The comments to this section note the importance of good faith as defined in the Uniform Commercial Code and as referenced in this section. Shortening the product review timeframe to 3 days could lead to a court finding that the agreement is unconscionable or not made in good faith, violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing that exists in all California contracts.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the product review timeframe from 15 to 3 days, creating an inconsistency regarding a reasonable timeframe for inspection. The Perturbation Explanation argues that 3 days might not allow sufficient time for proper evaluation, potentially harming KI, Inc.'s ability to protect its brand, and thus could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation supports this argument stating that such a short timeframe may lead to a court finding that the agreement is unconscionable or not made in good faith. Snippet 1 defines 'agreement' in California Commercial Code § 1201, highlighting that an agreement includes what can be inferred from the circumstances. Shortening the review period could be interpreted as acting without good faith, thus contradicting the implied covenant requirements. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition because the 3 day deadline could violate the state law regarding good faith and fair dealing.

perturbation.original_text: In the event books of account and records shall be kept available for at least two (2) years after the termination of this license.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event books of account and records shall be kept available for at least six (6) months after the termination of this license.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping duration to six months after termination conflicts with the general four-year statute of limitations for breach of written contract claims in California. This shorter period could hinder KI, Inc.'s ability to pursue legal action if discrepancies are discovered later.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. Reducing the record retention period to six months limits KI, Inc.'s ability to effectively investigate potential breaches and pursue legal remedies within the statutory timeframe, potentially waiving any legal remedy for the breach.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the record-keeping duration from two years to six months, directly contradicting the four-year statute of limitations for written contracts under California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing...'. By limiting the record retention period, KI, Inc.'s ability to effectively investigate breaches and pursue legal remedies within the statutory timeframe is impaired, making the term unenforceable.
